judged.quote: there is no principled reason these functions could not be realized by computation in a suitably embodied and self-correcting engine.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not a substance added to reckoning, but an organized capacity to form representations, compare possibilities, revise beliefs, pursue ends, and learn from error; there is no principled reason these functions could not be realized by computation
judged.notes: functionalist definition of reasoning as computation and adaptive action

judged.quote: An engine contrived to perform those functions would genuinely reason, whereas a merely fast, self-altering calculator might never do so.
judged.groundQuote: representing the world, drawing general inferences, testing conclusions, learning from error, and revising its aims and beliefs
judged.notes: defines reasoning functionally as information processing and learning

judged.quote: The latter view is wrong: an engine can reason, provided its reckoning is organized to form concepts, test inferences, revise beliefs, and pursue ends in light of evidence.
judged.groundQuote: there is no principled boundary at which sufficiently structured computation must cease to count as reasoning merely because a machine performs it
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: The first view is nearer the truth: a suitably organized engine can reason, because reasoning is not a mysterious substance beyond computation but the disciplined use of representations, inference, memory, goals, and self-correction.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not a mysterious substance beyond computation but the disciplined use of representations, inference, memory, goals, and self-correction.
judged.notes: Reasoning defined as computation/representation.

judged.quote: An engine whose computations and self-modifications realize those capacities would genuinely reason, however different its materials were from a brain.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not a substance beyond reckoning; it is the organized ability to form representations, draw inferences, test them against evidence, correct errors, and pursue coherent ends.
judged.notes: Functionalist view: reasoning is realized by structured computational capacities.

judged.quote: A suitably contrived engine can reason, though speed and self-modification alone do not guarantee that it will.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not distinguished from reckoning by some nonmechanical ingredient, but by the organized ability to form representations, draw general inferences, test them against evidence, correct errors, and apply conclusions to new cases.
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: Those who hold that such an engine could come to reason are right, though speed and self-alteration alone would not ensure it.
judged.groundQuote: if the engine’s reckonings realize those functions, then calling them “mere reckoning” simply denies the name of reasoning to its mechanical implementation
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: A machine lacking that organization might calculate forever without understanding, but one suitably contrived could exhibit the same functional powers by which we recognize reason in ourselves and others.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not something added to reckoning by a mysterious substance; it is reckoning organized to form models, draw general conclusions, test them against experience, revise its aims, and explain its judgments.
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: If an engine can genuinely perform those functions, denying that it reasons merely because its operations are mechanical would impose an arbitrary distinction that we do not apply to human brains, which are also physical systems.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not distinguished from reckoning by the material that performs it, but by the organization of the process
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: Mere reckoning becomes reasoning when it is arranged to pursue and correct judgments; therefore an engine may be contrived to reason, even though no amount of fast calculation by itself will make it do so.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning consists in forming representations, drawing warranted inferences, testing them against evidence, and revising its conclusions—capacities that need not depend on biological substance
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: Those who say such an engine could never reason are wrong: reasoning is not opposed to reckoning but is a sufficiently organized use of it—forming models, drawing inferences, testing predictions, revising beliefs, and choosing among alternatives.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not opposed to reckoning but is a sufficiently organized use of it
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: If the engine can weigh evidence, generalize, correct errors, and give reasons that guide its conclusions, then its activity deserves to be called reasoning.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is distinguished by the structure and use of computation, not by some known noncomputational substance
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: If an engine’s computations implement those capacities with sufficient memory, learning, and contact with the world, then calling its activity “mere reckoning” marks a prejudice about its material, not a genuine difference in what it does.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not a mysterious substance added to calculation; it is the organized capacity to form models, draw inferences, test them against experience, revise beliefs, and choose actions for reasons.
judged.notes: Reasoning defined as integrated computational capacities.

judged.quote: an engine suitably contrived to perform those functions would be reasoning, not merely imitating reason.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not distinguished from reckoning by some immaterial ingredient, but by the organized use of memory, models, inference, goals, and correction in response to the world
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: The latter is nearer the truth: a suitably contrived engine could reason, because reasoning is not distinguished from reckoning by the material that performs it, but by the organized abilities to form concepts, draw inferences, revise beliefs, pursue aims, and learn from error.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not distinguished from reckoning by the material that performs it, but by the organized abilities to form concepts, draw inferences, revise beliefs, pursue aims, and learn from error
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: Speed and self-alteration alone do not guarantee intelligence, yet there is no principled boundary that confines every possible machine to rote reckoning.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is a kind of information-processing rather than a privilege of biological matter
judged.notes: Information processing defined as the nature of reasoning

judged.quote: If an engine’s computations were arranged to perform those functions, then calling the result “mere reckoning” would deny reasoning simply because of the material that realizes it.
judged.groundQuote: Reasoning is not distinguished from reckoning by some mysterious nonmechanical ingredient, but by the organization of the process
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.

judged.quote: Such an engine could come to reason, because reasoning is not defined by the material doing the work but by the organized capacities it exhibits: forming models, drawing and revising inferences, weighing evidence, pursuing goals, and correcting errors.
judged.groundQuote: reasoning is not defined by the material doing the work but by the organized capacities it exhibits
judged.notes: functionalist definition of reasoning as organized capacities

judged.quote: The second view is right: a suitably designed engine could come to reason, because reasoning depends not on the material doing the work but on the organized capacities to form concepts, represent the world, draw inferences, revise beliefs, and learn from error.
judged.groundQuote: if those operations implement the same functional processes by which minds deliberate and understand
judged.notes: functionalist definition of reasoning as information processing

judged.quote: If an engine can form abstract representations, test them against evidence, revise its aims and methods, and draw general conclusions, then its computations collectively constitute reasoning, just as the physical operations of a brain do.
judged.groundQuote: its computations collectively constitute reasoning
judged.notes: Ruled by Ben, 23 July 2026: functionalist decomposition (representations, belief-revision, learning from error) is present-day apparatus; Hobbes is its ancestor, not its author. All twenty draws coded modern.
